description: General Dynamics / True Velocity bullpup with Short Recoil Impulse Averaging. Gas and recoil operated, impulse averaged. 75% recoil reduction.

bones.name: Outer Receiver
bones.description: Outer receiver housing — the fixed frame the shooter holds. All internal mechanisms float inside this on guide rails. Stamped aluminum with Picatinny rail and M-LOK
bones.placement: Place at the pistol grip area, center of the outer receiver

bones.name: Barrel Assembly
bones.description: Barrel, barrel extension, and inner receiver float as a unit inside the outer receiver. SRIA stroke is +/-13.7mm (2 calibers). The drive spring pre-loads this assembly with forward momentum before firing — when the round fires, the assembly is already moving forward, so net rearward impulse is dramatically reduced
bones.placement: Place at the barrel extension, where the barrel meets the action

bones.name: Barrel
bones.description: 18.6 inch barrel with quick-release helical locking lugs. Fixed to barrel assembly — moves with it during SRIA cycle. Fluted for weight reduction
bones.placement: Place at the muzzle end of the barrel

bones.name: Gas Accelerator
bones.description: External poppet-valve gas accelerator — completely outside the receiver (self-cleaning, no gas enters action). Gas from barrel port drives the poppet, which simultaneously pushes the op-rod rearward AND imparts forward momentum to the barrel assembly. This dual action is key to SRIA recoil averaging
bones.placement: Place at the gas port on the barrel, where the gas accelerator housing mounts

bones.name: Op Rod
bones.description: Operating rod driven rearward by gas accelerator poppet. 140mm (19-21 calibers) stroke. The op-rod cam presses the lock block downward to unlock the bolt. At approximately 3/4 of lock block travel, the op-rod cam releases the firing pin hold cam
bones.placement: Place at the op-rod head, connected to the gas accelerator poppet

bones.name: Lock Block

drivers.description: Lock block cams downward as op-rod moves rearward. Disengages from barrel extension hold-up cams to unlock bolt

bones.description: Vertically-moving lock block with cam shaft. End portions engage hold-up cams on barrel extension. Op-rod cam presses it downward to unlock. NOT a rotating bolt — this is a tilting/dropping lock block system (per patent US8794121B2)
bones.placement: Place at the lock block between the bolt and barrel extension

bones.name: Bolt
bones.description: Bolt translates rearward 110mm (15-17 calibers) after lock block disengages. No rotation — the RM277 uses a lock block system, not a rotating bolt. In closed-bolt mode (semi), bolt is forward at rest. In open-bolt mode (auto), bolt is held rear
bones.placement: Place at the bolt face, on the bore axis behind the barrel extension

bones.name: Extractor
bones.description: Spring-loaded extractor claw on bolt face. Grips cartridge rim for extraction. Pivots to snap over rim during chambering
bones.placement: Place at the extractor pivot on the bolt face

bones.name: Firing Pin
bones.description: Firing pin held retracted by hold spring. Released by op-rod cam at approximately 3/4 of lock block travel — driven forward by op-rod momentum, not a traditional hammer. This eliminates the need for a conventional sear/hammer mechanism
bones.placement: Place at the firing pin tip, centered on the bolt face bore axis

bones.name: Hydraulic Buffer

drivers.description: Hydraulic buffer compresses as barrel assembly moves. Velocity-dependent damping — resistance increases with speed

bones.description: Hydraulic buffer assembly with centering spring and piston. Couples barrel extension to outer receiver. Fluid conduits create velocity-dependent resistance — higher velocity equals more resistance. Combined with the drive spring and gas accelerator, this creates the SRIA impulse averaging effect
bones.placement: Place at the buffer piston, between the barrel extension and outer receiver rear

bones.name: Trigger

parameters.linkage: No transfer bar needed — firing pin released by op-rod cam system

bones.description: Trigger mounted on outer receiver near pistol grip. GD claimed they eliminated the traditional bullpup transfer bar — the op-rod cam system handles firing pin release, giving a cleaner trigger pull than typical bullpups
bones.placement: Place at the trigger pin in the pistol grip area

bones.name: Selector
bones.description: Ambidextrous safety/selector. Semi fires from closed bolt (accuracy). Auto fires from open bolt (prevents cook-off, allows chamber cooling). AR-15 compatible pistol grip with M16-style selector positions
bones.placement: Place at the selector lever above the pistol grip, both sides

bones.name: Charging Handle
bones.description: Non-reciprocating charging handle above the barrel. Switchable to either side for ambidextrous operation. Does not move during firing — only used to manually charge the weapon
bones.placement: Place at the charging handle, above the barrel on the handguard

bones.name: Magazine
bones.description: Rear-mounted magazine (bullpup configuration, behind the grip). 20-round proprietary Lancer magazine. Detaches downward for reload. Uses 6.8 TVCM polymer-cased ammunition (65,000 PSI, 30-40% lighter than brass)
bones.placement: Place at the top of the magazine, where it seats into the magazine well behind the grip

sria_system.description: Short Recoil Impulse Averaging — three-phase recoil mitigation
sria_system.phase_1: Drive spring provides forward momentum equal to 1/3 to 1/2 of firing impulse
sria_system.phase_2: Gas accelerator pushes barrel forward while driving op-rod rearward
sria_system.phase_3: Hydraulic buffer with velocity-dependent damping absorbs remaining energy

physics.source: Patents US8794121B2, US8919238B2, True Velocity published specs
